Bearing 315

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest | May/June 1999

[Image: A stylized, high-contrast graphic in the style of Pacific Northwest Native American art. It depicts a raven or bird wearing a compass around its neck, positioned next to a standard diagonally divided orienteering control flag.]

Cascade and Sammamish Orienteering Clubs would like to extend a special invitation to the three-day

1999 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival

this July 23-25, at Cle Elum, WA

Our Perspective
Our focus has always been on hosting a top-notch yet fun orienteering festival. After some additional feedback, we have redesigned our event to better meet this goal and your interest.

Event Overview
Friday’s event will be on COC’s Teanaway Forks map, which was produced in 1997-98 for the 1998 US Classic Course Championships. Friday will be a stand-alone A-meet. Lick Creek, with the map also produced in 1997-98, is the venue for COC’s Saturday’s event, which is day one of the two-day combined time event. On Sunday, Sammamish OC will serve as the host for the final day of the two-day combined time event. This will take place at Bullfrog Pond. This map was produced for the 1995 Western State Championships. Our course setters for the festival are Peter Golde, Don Denbo, and Jan Urban, respectively. Lastly, while all of the venues are runnable, each offers a different feel under foot and to the orienteer’s eye.

The Terrain – A visual approach
Pictures of the terrain are located on the event website (address is listed below). We are also placing representative areas of each map at this site. All maps are 1:15,000 scale with 5-meter contours.

Course Options, Classes, and Awards
The complete USOF course and class selections are offered each day. For classes with 5 or more competitors, medals will be awarded to the top three finishers of the Saturday/Sunday combined time event.

Start Times
Start times on each of the three days are scheduled between 9am to 11am, partly in an effort to reduce the warm summer weather complications. Start times will appear on the event website a few days before the event.

Registration and Contact Information
Event website: http://students.washington.edu/run/pnwof99
Meet Directors:
Friday: Rick Hood, 425-640-8134, rickhood@gte.net
Saturday & Sunday: Dave Tallent, 425-488-3691, dtallent@gtemail.net

Make checks payable to “COC” and mail completed entries to:

Bill Cusworth, PNWOF Registrar
7811 3rd Ave NW
Seattle, WA 98117-4006

The Registrar can also be reached at bill3@halcyon.com; 206-789-1346.

In This Issue:
Upcoming Events 1
Training 3
Letters 3
Meet Results 3

Copyright 1999, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club. Submissions for the next issue must be received by June 25, 1999. Send submissions to Don Denbo at dwd@halcyon.com or mail to 2138 N 186th St., Shoreline, WA, 98133.

Upcoming Events

Washington State Orienteering Champs

June 5-6, 1999
Brooks Memorial State Park
Goldendale, Washington
The site of the 1998 NIKE World Masters Games orienteering competition this year welcomes the Washington State Orienteering Association championships, hosted by the Columbia River Orienteering Club. This two-day “classic” event will feature competitors from all around the Northwest.

The event site, generously made available by Boise Cascade, was mapped in 1997 by George Kirkov. Being on the dry side of the Cascade Mountains, and on the edge of the high desert, this “yellow pine biotic zone” includes second growth ponderosa pine and oak forest, with some meadows. It’s quite open and runnable and should be fairly fast. The main hazards are possible high temperatures and ground covered with small loose volcanic rocks from ancient Cascade eruptions. Meet director Glen Cafferty says that water will be on the courses but everyone would be well advised to carry their own.

Registration
Deadline for entries is May 15. Please submit your entry by May 15 to avoid the late fee. If you E-mail your registration before May 15, you have until May 28 to mail the signed waiver and the entry fees. Contact Peter Kehoe at kehoep@pacifier.com or Glen Cafferty at 360-253-5129 for entry forms.

Meet Information
Awards will be given out to the top finishers in each class based on the combined two-day total time. In addition, the Washington State Champion will be awarded the top finisher from Washington in each class.

A non-competitive Maphike class will be available for groups or individuals on the White, Yellow and Orange courses.

How To Get There
From Portland:

Drive east on Interstate-84 beyond The Dalles to US Highway 97 (exit is 96 miles from the I-84/I-205 interchange). Drive north on Highway 97 26 miles to Brooks Memorial State Park.

Or take the scenic route, about the same distance as the I-84 route but on a slower highway: Drive east on Washington Highway 14 to Washington Highway 142 (70 miles east of the I-205/Hwy 14 interchange in Vancouver). Drive north on Highway 142 through Klickitat then east to Goldendale (35 miles). Turn north on US Highway 97 and drive 11 miles to Brooks Memorial State Park.

From Central Washington:

Drive south on US Highway 97 to Brooks Memorial State Park, 35 miles south of the intersection of US Highway 97 and Washington Highway 22 in Toppenish.

Newcastle Island

May 29 and 30, 1999
A Two Day B Meet
1998 was the first time in 23 years that there was no orienteering on Newcastle Island. WELL, WE ARE BACK IN 1999.

The Newcastle Island map (1996) is a 5 colour, IOF standard; 1:10 000; 5 metre contour map. Expect a little bush, a lot of open forest, some detailed terrain and lots of rock features.

Course Planner: Steve Chatwin
Meet Director: Marvin Eng

Starts:
1:00 - 3:00 pm, May 29
10:00 - 11:00 am, May 30.

Courses, Categories & Awards
Hey, this is “just” a B-Meet. We will offer 5 courses each day. There will be no pre-drawn maps so you, and the runners you like to compete against, can have a look at the master maps and pick the one you like. There will be awards for the best male and female 2-day total time on each course. The only wrinkle is that course 1 and course 2 awards will only be given to people under 13 and under 15, respectively.

To Pre-Register
Contact Marvin Eng [(250) 652-9115 or eng@islandnet.com] or send a cheque (payable to the Victorienteers) to 7268 Veyaness Rd, Saanicton, BC, V8M 1M2. If you pre-register by phone or E-mail and don’t show up you go on the “bad person” list.

The deadline is Friday, May 21, 1999

Saturday Social
Why pre-register for a B Meet? Because this year we are returning to a Newcastle "tradition" - the oyster dinner on Saturday night. For $5 you get oysters cooked the outdoorsy way, corn on the cob, Caesar salad, and a bun. But we need to know how many oysters to buy. So please pre-register (you also get a break on the meet fees - see below).

Fees
Per day for members (VO, COC, USOF):

On Site Pre-Registered Oyster Dinner
Adults (20+) $10 $8 $5
Juniors $5 $4 $3
Family $20 $16 $15

Non-members add $2 per person per day.

Accommodation
Group camping will be available. Follow the signs once you get off the ferry to Newcastle Island. ($2 per person per night collected on site). Motel/Hotel accommodation is available in Nanaimo (phone 1-800-663-7337).

To Get There
From Departure Bay (Vancouver ferry) turn left onto the old Island Highway, to downtown Nanaimo, and left at the first set of lights onto Comox Rd. From Victoria go all the way through downtown and turn right onto Comox Rd. Once on Comox Rd. take an immediate left into the Civic Arena parking lot. The ferry to Newcastle Island leaves from the east end of the parking lot (plus a bit of walk); there are directions and parking instructions when you enter the parking lot. Departures are hourly on the hour form 10:00 am to 7:00 pm - $4.75 adults, $3.75 children and seniors; round trip.

1999 Classic Distance US Orienteering Championships

A two-day USOF sanctioned A-meet
September 25 and 26, 1999

Lake Tahoe-Nevada State Park at Spooner Lake
The Lake Tahoe basin, located in the center of the Sierra Nevada mountain range, is one of the most scenic areas in the United States. The Lake Tahoe-Nevada State Park at Spooner Lake, located on the eastern side of the basin, overlooks all of Lake Tahoe and the mountains that surround the area. The park is an alpine fir forest with scattered small clearings, few trails, lots of rock features, and lots of boulder fields to challenge your navigational skills as well as those of your competitors. At 7000 feet in elevation, the occasional views will be stupendous, the forest will be mostly open and runnable, you'll find no poison oak, and nobody has seen rattlesnakes anywhere in the basin.

In late September, the average mean high temperature is 65 degrees and the mean low temperature is 44 degrees with a minimal chance for rain or snow. Still the weather in the Sierra is variable and often unpredictable-so be prepared for anything from 80°F in the sun to freezing temperatures in the snow.

The map you'll use is the first IOF-standard map used for an A-meet in Nevada. This US Championships will be the first use of this map. You've seen the forests if you've watched Bonanza on TV (filmed just four miles from Spooner Lake).

Courses/Awards
This event includes all standard courses for USOF classes. There will also be recreational courses (WYO), beginner's clinics, and Start-O courses on both days.

How to Register
To register by mail, use a standard USOF entry form (available in the Jan. issue of ONA or on the BAOC web site http://www.baoc.org) and send it with your check (payable to BAOC) to: Marsha Jacobs 501 Roosevelt Blvd., Half Moon Bay, CA 94019-1341 650-726-6636 (9am-9pm Pacific time only)

To register by e-mail, include your registration information (follow the form) in an e-mail message and send it to marsha@batnet.com. Be sure to mail your check (payable to BAOC) to the above address. Your registration is not complete until BAOC receives your check. Register by September 1 to avoid paying a late fee.

Training

Harvey Friedman

Producing a Local O' Meet — Event Administration Class — a Practical How-To
Cascade Orienteering Club needs more people willing to be involved in furthering our sport. This course is a good opportunity for newcomers, or long-time club members to learn what is involved, and to meet people who you might have only seen in the woods.

As a certificated Level I & 2 COF controller for the past ten years, I propose a course to instruct Cascade members on what is optimal for a local meet, including preparation, publicity, meet workers (number of, and specific duties for each), post meet cleanup, post meet results, publicity, etc.

I will put together information from the USOF club binder, the COF Level I controller's course, Debbie Newell's manual for local meet directors, and the over 15 years of experience I have had organizing O' events locally and participating locally, nationally, and internationally.

To make sure everything has time for questions, only the first ten people will be accepted for this class. Handouts will be provided at my copying costs, probably less than $2. Phone me evenings between 6:30-9:30 at 206-784-2774 to sign up or for more information and to select a date mutually agreeable for the majority of students and for me.

Letters

Newcomer's Experience
Hey, well I was finally able to attend an orienteering event. I went to the newcomers meet at Woodland Park yesterday. I had a great time and was glad that I was able to receive some help and instruction from some of the people putting on the event (you included). I even joined the club when I finished all three courses.
Thanks again!
— Douglas Williams

Where's My Map?
(reprinted from ONA)

Dear ONA,
In November 1977, I traveled with Tony Byrne of Vancouver to Eugene, Oregon to take in what was billed as a two-day A meet (the first in Oregon). As I recall, the terrain was all right, but the meet quality was not the greatest. One peculiar aspect of the meet was that the meet organizers never did hand back the maps after collecting them on the second day, and I have wondered about that ever since. Can any of your readers help me out by recalling details of the infamous meet - such as who some of the organizers were and where I might be able to track down a copy of the map, if any exist? I would gladly pay for a color copy of the map if anyone has a copy of the original. It was a color map. I can't recall the name of the area but I think it was right on the outskirts of Eugene, to the southeast.
Thanks.
— Ross Burnett, 190 Rainbow Rd, Whitehorse, Yukon Y1A 5E2, Canada, phone: 867-667-5030, fax: 867-393-6213

Meet Results

Bennington Lake

March 21, 1999
Walla Walla, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers (SACO)

Meet Director and Course Setter: Frank Skorina
Control Pickup: Steve Rapp
Participants: 17

That weather can bite. After such a nice Saturday that I even got sunburned, Sunday was cloudy, then windy, and then rainy. It did not clear up until control pickup time. Nevertheless, the hearty were there, braving our first meet of the season.

Despite the weather, this is actually a great time of the year for orienteering. Vegetation has not had a chance to proliferate so it is easy running through the grasses. As the first meet of the year, I made the courses a little on the short and easy side, and with long legs on the advanced course. I gave the option of a super long course by combining the long and medium courses, but did not have any takers.

Greg VanDonge became a SACO member last week just after our schedule mailing, without having participated in a meet, so he thought he should try one this weekend. He went out with his son and his son's pal. They liked it so much that they decided to go out again, this time with the addition of Greg's daughter from Independence, OR and her man.

Simultaneous to our event was mounted orienteering, as in horses. In MO, participants use a copy of a USGS map with a number of large, 200-300 meter circles on it. Somewhere, not necessarily in the center, is a paper plate that can be seen on horseback that participants are searching for. For each circle there is also a set of three clues. One clue may be "17 degrees from the tall tree with the woodpecker holes." The intersection of the bearings of these three clues give the location of the paper plate. We saw about 20 sauntering ponies and even gave out a couple of schedules to the friendly, wet riders. No collisions between the groups of orienteers were reported.

Sacajawea Park

March 27-28, 1999
Pasco, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers (SACO)

Meet Directory, Course Setter, and Reporter: Ivar Husa

We had a great weekend for orienteering at Sacajawea Park. We had an excellent turn out, 32 participants, with four people running two courses. The weather was on the cool and windy side, but wound up on Sunday with light wind, overcast and temperatures in the 50's. That is pretty near ideal for a runner.

We put out our usual beginner, intermediate, and advanced courses, with the same courses both Saturday and Sunday afternoons. We tried to make it as easy as possible to participate. Several bald eagles were spotted, and one golden eagle with a 2+ meter wingspan. (Advanced course time includes time to copy course)

Howard Amon Park

April 10, 1999
Richland, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers (SACO)

Meet Director: Ivar Husa
Course Setters: Steve Weakley and Ivar Husa
Registration, Start, and Finish: Peggy Higham
Participants: 29

The fine, sunny weather brought us a large, patient group of participants. This was an intro-level event in a city park with three courses. We re-marked maps, allowing participants to cover all three courses at the bargain price of $3. That's $1 per course--call us the Earl Scheib(1) of Orienteering. This format resulted in more "course participants" than any other SACO event. Nearly everyone ran all Weakley's courses, for a total distance of 4.6 km, in his course setting debut.

Many efforts have been made in the recording and compilation of these results. If you believe there is an error, please E-mail me and I will correct them lickety-split.

(1) Earl Scheib are the guys who pelted the airwaves a number of years ago with offers to paint your car for something like $20.

Woodland Park

Seattle, WA
Cascade Orienteering Club

We had a good Newcomers' event thanks to both a strong turn out of first time orienteers and willing meet staff on Saturday the 10th of April at Woodland Park. (Having it NOT rain didn't hurt either.)

I heard from Scott Drumm the CROC had a super Newcomers' event in the Portland area as well, drawing about 130 individuals.

What follows is an listing of those that helped and those that participated at COC's Woodland Park event. Thanks to one and all!!!

Pioneer Park

April 11, 1999
Walla Walla, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers (SACO)

Meet Director: Frank Skorina
Course Setters: Darci and Dan Hamilton, Frank Skorina
Map Copying: Sonny Elsasser
Instruction: Ivar Husa
Control Pickup: Midge Crawford, Kit Crawford, Tom Kelleher
Participants: 27

The weather again cooperated for the second day of the orienteering newcomers' weekend for several clubs in the northwest. The turnout was almost as good as yesterday's meet at Howard Amon park, thanks to Scott Shafer and 12 of his students from his sophomore physical education course at Walla Walla Valley Academy.

None of Shafer's athletic students could top the pair of Pete Crawford, Jr. and John B. The Crawford family have been trying to get to a meet for perhaps years, made up for lost time by running a total of 13 courses. But the Pete and John pair could not beat Sonny Elsasser on the long course. He left his "cowboy boots" at home today and ran with hiking boots, smiling and waving each time the long course passed by the start/finish table.

A special thanks to Darci and Dan Hamilton who set their first courses.

Wallula Junction

April 18, 1999
Pasco, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers (SACO)

Course Setter and Reporter: Ivar Husa
Control pickup, Rick McCain, Bob Gruel, Frank Skorina

'Twas a lovely day in the field (high overcast, temperatures in the 70's) for the 14 ambitious orienteers. The complex surface features and a map that is 'rough' in places gave participants on each course navigational challenges. Rough map? Well, certain areas were inaccessible swamp when mapping took place 2 years ago, and much if it was mapped as 'fight', and is now navigable. This event used some of this terrain, so there was an element of 'unknown' that is usually missing (on a good map!).

Two 'second-time participant' families liked the event well enough to join the club. Welcome to the Rick McCain family (with Mike) of Richland and Pete Crawford family (with Kit) from Walla Walla. Rick and Mike were the only ones to run both the Intermediate and Advanced courses. Rick edged son (Mike) by 8 minutes on the combined total. Maybe next time, Mike!

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

Bearing 315 is Published by:

Cascade Orienteering Club
Post Office Box 31375
Seattle, Washington 98103
(206) 783-3866

block_index.text: The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest | May/June 1999

[Image: A stylized, high-contrast graphic in the style of Pacific Northwest Native American art. It depicts a raven or bird wearing a compass around its neck, positioned next to a standard diagonally divided orienteering control flag.]
Cascade and Sammamish Orienteering Clubs would like to extend a special invitation to the three-day
this July 23-25, at Cle Elum, WA

Our Perspective Our focus has always been on hosting a top-notch yet fun orienteering festival. After some additional feedback, we have redesigned our event to better meet this goal and your interest.

Event Overview Friday’s event will be on COC’s Teanaway Forks map, which was produced in 1997-98 for the 1998 US Classic Course Championships. Friday will be a stand-alone A-meet. Lick Creek, with the map also produced in 1997-98, is the venue for COC’s Saturday’s event, which is day one of the two-day combined time event. On Sunday, Sammamish OC will serve as the host for the final day of the two-day combined time event. This will take place at Bullfrog Pond. This map was produced for the 1995 Western State Championships. Our course setters for the festival are Peter Golde, Don Denbo, and Jan Urban, respectively. Lastly, while all of the venues are runnable, each offers a different feel under foot and to the orienteer’s eye.

The Terrain – A visual approach Pictures of the terrain are located on the event website (address is listed below). We are also placing representative areas of each map at this site. All maps are 1:15,000 scale with 5-meter contours.

Course Options, Classes, and Awards The complete USOF course and class selections are offered each day. For classes with 5 or more competitors, medals will be awarded to the top three finishers of the Saturday/Sunday combined time event.

Start Times Start times on each of the three days are scheduled between 9am to 11am, partly in an effort to reduce the warm summer weather complications. Start times will appear on the event website a few days before the event.

Registration and Contact Information Event website: http://students.washington.edu/run/pnwof99 Meet Directors: Friday: Rick Hood, 425-640-8134, rickhood@gte.net Saturday & Sunday: Dave Tallent, 425-488-3691, dtallent@gtemail.net
Make checks payable to “COC” and mail completed entries to:
Bill Cusworth, PNWOF Registrar 7811 3rd Ave NW Seattle, WA 98117-4006
The Registrar can also be reached at bill3@halcyon.com; 206-789-1346.

Copyright 1999, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club. Submissions for the next issue must be received by June 25, 1999. Send submissions to Don Denbo at dwd@halcyon.com or mail to 2138 N 186th St., Shoreline, WA, 98133.

June 5-6, 1999 Brooks Memorial State Park Goldendale, Washington The site of the 1998 NIKE World Masters Games orienteering competition this year welcomes the Washington State Orienteering Association championships, hosted by the Columbia River Orienteering Club. This two-day “classic” event will feature competitors from all around the Northwest.

The event site, generously made available by Boise Cascade, was mapped in 1997 by George Kirkov. Being on the dry side of the Cascade Mountains, and on the edge of the high desert, this “yellow pine biotic zone” includes second growth ponderosa pine and oak forest, with some meadows. It’s quite open and runnable and should be fairly fast. The main hazards are possible high temperatures and ground covered with small loose volcanic rocks from ancient Cascade eruptions. Meet director Glen Cafferty says that water will be on the courses but everyone would be well advised to carry their own.

Registration Deadline for entries is May 15. Please submit your entry by May 15 to avoid the late fee. If you E-mail your registration before May 15, you have until May 28 to mail the signed waiver and the entry fees. Contact Peter Kehoe at kehoep@pacifier.com or Glen Cafferty at 360-253-5129 for entry forms.

Meet Information Awards will be given out to the top finishers in each class based on the combined two-day total time. In addition, the Washington State Champion will be awarded the top finisher from Washington in each class.
A non-competitive Maphike class will be available for groups or individuals on the White, Yellow and Orange courses.
How To Get There From Portland:
Drive east on Interstate-84 beyond The Dalles to US Highway 97 (exit is 96 miles from the I-84/I-205 interchange). Drive north on Highway 97 26 miles to Brooks Memorial State Park.

Or take the scenic route, about the same distance as the I-84 route but on a slower highway: Drive east on Washington Highway 14 to Washington Highway 142 (70 miles east of the I-205/Hwy 14 interchange in Vancouver). Drive north on Highway 142 through Klickitat then east to Goldendale (35 miles). Turn north on US Highway 97 and drive 11 miles to Brooks Memorial State Park.
Drive south on US Highway 97 to Brooks Memorial State Park, 35 miles south of the intersection of US Highway 97 and Washington Highway 22 in Toppenish.
May 29 and 30, 1999 A Two Day B Meet 1998 was the first time in 23 years that there was no orienteering on Newcastle Island. WELL, WE ARE BACK IN 1999.
The Newcastle Island map (1996) is a 5 colour, IOF standard; 1:10 000; 5 metre contour map. Expect a little bush, a lot of open forest, some detailed terrain and lots of rock features.
Course Planner: Steve Chatwin Meet Director: Marvin Eng

Courses, Categories & Awards Hey, this is “just” a B-Meet. We will offer 5 courses each day. There will be no pre-drawn maps so you, and the runners you like to compete against, can have a look at the master maps and pick the one you like. There will be awards for the best male and female 2-day total time on each course. The only wrinkle is that course 1 and course 2 awards will only be given to people under 13 and under 15, respectively.

To Pre-Register Contact Marvin Eng [(250) 652-9115 or eng@islandnet.com] or send a cheque (payable to the Victorienteers) to 7268 Veyaness Rd, Saanicton, BC, V8M 1M2. If you pre-register by phone or E-mail and don’t show up you go on the “bad person” list.

Saturday Social Why pre-register for a B Meet? Because this year we are returning to a Newcastle "tradition" - the oyster dinner on Saturday night. For $5 you get oysters cooked the outdoorsy way, corn on the cob, Caesar salad, and a bun. But we need to know how many oysters to buy. So please pre-register (you also get a break on the meet fees - see below).
Fees Per day for members (VO, COC, USOF):
Non-members add $2 per person per day.

Accommodation Group camping will be available. Follow the signs once you get off the ferry to Newcastle Island. ($2 per person per night collected on site). Motel/Hotel accommodation is available in Nanaimo (phone 1-800-663-7337).

To Get There From Departure Bay (Vancouver ferry) turn left onto the old Island Highway, to downtown Nanaimo, and left at the first set of lights onto Comox Rd. From Victoria go all the way through downtown and turn right onto Comox Rd. Once on Comox Rd. take an immediate left into the Civic Arena parking lot. The ferry to Newcastle Island leaves from the east end of the parking lot (plus a bit of walk); there are directions and parking instructions when you enter the parking lot. Departures are hourly on the hour form 10:00 am to 7:00 pm - $4.75 adults, $3.75 children and seniors; round trip.
A two-day USOF sanctioned A-meet September 25 and 26, 1999

Lake Tahoe-Nevada State Park at Spooner Lake The Lake Tahoe basin, located in the center of the Sierra Nevada mountain range, is one of the most scenic areas in the United States. The Lake Tahoe-Nevada State Park at Spooner Lake, located on the eastern side of the basin, overlooks all of Lake Tahoe and the mountains that surround the area. The park is an alpine fir forest with scattered small clearings, few trails, lots of rock features, and lots of boulder fields to challenge your navigational skills as well as those of your competitors. At 7000 feet in elevation, the occasional views will be stupendous, the forest will be mostly open and runnable, you'll find no poison oak, and nobody has seen rattlesnakes anywhere in the basin.

In late September, the average mean high temperature is 65 degrees and the mean low temperature is 44 degrees with a minimal chance for rain or snow. Still the weather in the Sierra is variable and often unpredictable-so be prepared for anything from 80°F in the sun to freezing temperatures in the snow.

The map you'll use is the first IOF-standard map used for an A-meet in Nevada. This US Championships will be the first use of this map. You've seen the forests if you've watched Bonanza on TV (filmed just four miles from Spooner Lake).
Courses/Awards This event includes all standard courses for USOF classes. There will also be recreational courses (WYO), beginner's clinics, and Start-O courses on both days.

How to Register To register by mail, use a standard USOF entry form (available in the Jan. issue of ONA or on the BAOC web site http://www.baoc.org) and send it with your check (payable to BAOC) to: Marsha Jacobs 501 Roosevelt Blvd., Half Moon Bay, CA 94019-1341 650-726-6636 (9am-9pm Pacific time only)

To register by e-mail, include your registration information (follow the form) in an e-mail message and send it to marsha@batnet.com. Be sure to mail your check (payable to BAOC) to the above address. Your registration is not complete until BAOC receives your check. Register by September 1 to avoid paying a late fee.

Producing a Local O' Meet — Event Administration Class — a Practical How-To Cascade Orienteering Club needs more people willing to be involved in furthering our sport. This course is a good opportunity for newcomers, or long-time club members to learn what is involved, and to meet people who you might have only seen in the woods.

As a certificated Level I & 2 COF controller for the past ten years, I propose a course to instruct Cascade members on what is optimal for a local meet, including preparation, publicity, meet workers (number of, and specific duties for each), post meet cleanup, post meet results, publicity, etc.

I will put together information from the USOF club binder, the COF Level I controller's course, Debbie Newell's manual for local meet directors, and the over 15 years of experience I have had organizing O' events locally and participating locally, nationally, and internationally.

To make sure everything has time for questions, only the first ten people will be accepted for this class. Handouts will be provided at my copying costs, probably less than $2. Phone me evenings between 6:30-9:30 at 206-784-2774 to sign up or for more information and to select a date mutually agreeable for the majority of students and for me.

Newcomer's Experience Hey, well I was finally able to attend an orienteering event. I went to the newcomers meet at Woodland Park yesterday. I had a great time and was glad that I was able to receive some help and instruction from some of the people putting on the event (you included). I even joined the club when I finished all three courses. Thanks again! — Douglas Williams

Dear ONA, In November 1977, I traveled with Tony Byrne of Vancouver to Eugene, Oregon to take in what was billed as a two-day A meet (the first in Oregon). As I recall, the terrain was all right, but the meet quality was not the greatest. One peculiar aspect of the meet was that the meet organizers never did hand back the maps after collecting them on the second day, and I have wondered about that ever since. Can any of your readers help me out by recalling details of the infamous meet - such as who some of the organizers were and where I might be able to track down a copy of the map, if any exist? I would gladly pay for a color copy of the map if anyone has a copy of the original. It was a color map. I can't recall the name of the area but I think it was right on the outskirts of Eugene, to the southeast. Thanks. — Ross Burnett, 190 Rainbow Rd, Whitehorse, Yukon Y1A 5E2, Canada, phone: 867-667-5030, fax: 867-393-6213
March 21, 1999 Walla Walla, WA Sacajawea Orienteers (SACO)
Meet Director and Course Setter: Frank Skorina Control Pickup: Steve Rapp Participants: 17

That weather can bite. After such a nice Saturday that I even got sunburned, Sunday was cloudy, then windy, and then rainy. It did not clear up until control pickup time. Nevertheless, the hearty were there, braving our first meet of the season.

Despite the weather, this is actually a great time of the year for orienteering. Vegetation has not had a chance to proliferate so it is easy running through the grasses. As the first meet of the year, I made the courses a little on the short and easy side, and with long legs on the advanced course. I gave the option of a super long course by combining the long and medium courses, but did not have any takers.

Greg VanDonge became a SACO member last week just after our schedule mailing, without having participated in a meet, so he thought he should try one this weekend. He went out with his son and his son's pal. They liked it so much that they decided to go out again, this time with the addition of Greg's daughter from Independence, OR and her man.

Simultaneous to our event was mounted orienteering, as in horses. In MO, participants use a copy of a USGS map with a number of large, 200-300 meter circles on it. Somewhere, not necessarily in the center, is a paper plate that can be seen on horseback that participants are searching for. For each circle there is also a set of three clues. One clue may be "17 degrees from the tall tree with the woodpecker holes." The intersection of the bearings of these three clues give the location of the paper plate. We saw about 20 sauntering ponies and even gave out a couple of schedules to the friendly, wet riders. No collisions between the groups of orienteers were reported.
Greg and Daniel VanDonge, Hayden Rapp
Darci, Dan, & Zak Hamilton, Donna Kennedy
Greg and Daniel VanDonge, Hayden Rapp, Sara VanDonge and Bill Ross
Meet Directory, Course Setter, and Reporter: Ivar Husa

We had a great weekend for orienteering at Sacajawea Park. We had an excellent turn out, 32 participants, with four people running two courses. The weather was on the cool and windy side, but wound up on Sunday with light wind, overcast and temperatures in the 50's. That is pretty near ideal for a runner.

We put out our usual beginner, intermediate, and advanced courses, with the same courses both Saturday and Sunday afternoons. We tried to make it as easy as possible to participate. Several bald eagles were spotted, and one golden eagle with a 2+ meter wingspan. (Advanced course time includes time to copy course)
Steve, Diana, Emily Shankle and David Freund
Christine LeMaitre, Nicole Cournac, and Liliane Billaux
Meet Director: Ivar Husa Course Setters: Steve Weakley and Ivar Husa Registration, Start, and Finish: Peggy Higham Participants: 29

The fine, sunny weather brought us a large, patient group of participants. This was an intro-level event in a city park with three courses. We re-marked maps, allowing participants to cover all three courses at the bargain price of $3. That's $1 per course--call us the Earl Scheib(1) of Orienteering. This format resulted in more "course participants" than any other SACO event. Nearly everyone ran all Weakley's courses, for a total distance of 4.6 km, in his course setting debut.
Many efforts have been made in the recording and compilation of these results. If you believe there is an error, please E-mail me and I will correct them lickety-split.
(1) Earl Scheib are the guys who pelted the airwaves a number of years ago with offers to paint your car for something like $20.
April 10, 1999 Seattle, WA Cascade Orienteering Club

Eric Bone: Instruction/Marker Pick-Up Bill Cusworth: Finishes/Equipment Support Ardis Dull: Registration Harvey Friedman: Starts Rick Hood: Meet Director/Course Designer Bruce McAlister: Refreshments/Finishes Robert Miller: Course Vetter/Marker Hanger Debbie Newell: Instruction Dave Petty: Accounting Dave Tallent: Instruction Kevin Welton: Registration/Marker Pick-Up

We had a good Newcomers' event thanks to both a strong turn out of first time orienteers and willing meet staff on Saturday the 10th of April at Woodland Park. (Having it NOT rain didn't hurt either.)
I heard from Scott Drumm the CROC had a super Newcomers' event in the Portland area as well, drawing about 130 individuals.
What follows is an listing of those that helped and those that participated at COC's Woodland Park event. Thanks to one and all!!!
Event Participant List (Participants completing multiple course are indicated in parenthesis, based upon punch card results.)
April 11, 1999 Walla Walla, WA Sacajawea Orienteers (SACO)

Meet Director: Frank Skorina Course Setters: Darci and Dan Hamilton, Frank Skorina Map Copying: Sonny Elsasser Instruction: Ivar Husa Control Pickup: Midge Crawford, Kit Crawford, Tom Kelleher Participants: 27

The weather again cooperated for the second day of the orienteering newcomers' weekend for several clubs in the northwest. The turnout was almost as good as yesterday's meet at Howard Amon park, thanks to Scott Shafer and 12 of his students from his sophomore physical education course at Walla Walla Valley Academy.

None of Shafer's athletic students could top the pair of Pete Crawford, Jr. and John B. The Crawford family have been trying to get to a meet for perhaps years, made up for lost time by running a total of 13 courses. But the Pete and John pair could not beat Sonny Elsasser on the long course. He left his "cowboy boots" at home today and ran with hiking boots, smiling and waving each time the long course passed by the start/finish table.
A special thanks to Darci and Dan Hamilton who set their first courses.
Course Setter and Reporter: Ivar Husa Control pickup, Rick McCain, Bob Gruel, Frank Skorina

'Twas a lovely day in the field (high overcast, temperatures in the 70's) for the 14 ambitious orienteers. The complex surface features and a map that is 'rough' in places gave participants on each course navigational challenges. Rough map? Well, certain areas were inaccessible swamp when mapping took place 2 years ago, and much if it was mapped as 'fight', and is now navigable. This event used some of this terrain, so there was an element of 'unknown' that is usually missing (on a good map!).

Two 'second-time participant' families liked the event well enough to join the club. Welcome to the Rick McCain family (with Mike) of Richland and Pete Crawford family (with Kit) from Walla Walla. Rick and Mike were the only ones to run both the Intermediate and Advanced courses. Rick edged son (Mike) by 8 minutes on the combined total. Maybe next time, Mike!
Darci, Dan, Zak Hamilton & Donna Kennedy
The following summary was created from Bill Cusworth's rankings website. Apologies to anyone inadvertantly omitted.
Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.
map fee only after first two

Hamlin Park, Shoreline, WA. Exit I-5 at NE 145th. Travel east to 15th Ave NE. Turn north to NE 160th.
Washington Park, Anacortes, WA. Take the highway from Anacortes toward the San Juan ferries. Follow signs straight to park when ferry traffic turns right.
Horn Rapids, Richland, WA. Mt. Bike option. Take Highway 240, 9 miles from the "Bypass" Highway, NW of Richland or take Highway 225, 10 miles N of Benton City.
Ros*Elum, Roslyn, WA. Exit I-90 at Exit 80, then head north about 2 miles. Turn right towards Cle Elum on Highway 902. Watch for signs on left about 0.1 mile.

Liberty Lake County Park, Spokane, WA. Take I-90 east from Spokane (or west from Coeur D'Alene) to Exit 296 (Liberty Lake). East on Mission to Molter, then turn right and take Molter to Valley Way. Turn left and follow Valley Way onto Lakeside Road and follow the signs to the county park entrance.
Champoeg State Park, Newberg, OR. From I-5 or I-205 south of where they rejoin south of Portland, follow signs from I-5.
Ann Morrison Park, Boise, ID. Call for directions.
BC Champs (Sage Stomp) Stake Lake, Kamloops BC area.
Cornwall Park, Bellingham, WA. Exit I-5 at Exit 256A and head south on Meridian St. Travel for 0.5 miles and turn left at Squalicum Way into the park.

Howard Amon, Richland, WA. From Interstate 182 near Richland, WA take exit 5, selecting the ramp to go north on George Washington Way. Proceed 1 mile north and turn right (east) on Lee Blvd. One block ahead.
Robinswood Park, Bellevue, WA. Take 148th Ave. north form I-90, park is on east side of road.
Brooks State Park, Goldendale, WA. Washington State Champs. See directions in related article.
Lincoln Park, Spokane, WA. Call for registration location and directions.
Pioneer Park, Walla Walla, WA. From Rt 12 take Clinton Street South. At the second traffic light, hang a left. Go about 0.3 mile and you should see big trees on your right. That is the park.
Lincoln Park, Seattle, WA. Exit from I-5 at Spokane St. Head west until you reach Fauntleroy Way, head south to the park.
Julia Davis Park, Boise, ID. Call for directions.
Big Finn Hill, Bothell, WA. Call for directions.
Black Mtn Rogaine, W. Vancouver, BC.
Barebones Festival, Cranbrook, BC. "4-day, five-event, two-hotsprings, one lasagne feast"
Battleground Lk Training Camp, Battleground, WA.
Battleground Lk State Park, Battleground, WA. Travel to Battle Ground from I-5, drive slowly right through town, turn left (north) onto Grace Ave, follow signs.
Luther Burbank Park, Mercer Island, WA. Exit I-90 at the Island Crest Way exit. Travel north one block to 26th St., head east to park.

As a U.S. Citizen or U.S. Resident, I hereby apply for USOF membership. I understand that I am entitled to a one-year subscription to Orienteering North America, voting rights, and membership in the IOF. Please print.
Member of a chartered club? Club _________________

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
